claim: AI evals are the number one most important new skill for product managers in 2026, requiring systematic evaluation before shipping features to production.
actionable: Prioritize learning systematic eval methodology including observability, error analysis, and LLM judge design as a core PM competency.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Start eval work with observability and trace collection, not metrics - instrument your code first to capture execution traces from real usage.
actionable: Set up trace logging to CSV/JSON/observability tool before defining any metrics; log all turns and information shown to the LLM.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Error analysis (open coding - scanning and noting issues) is the most critical and most skipped step in evals; spending 30 seconds per trace on 100 traces yields deep system understanding.
actionable: Manually scan 100 production traces (30 seconds each) and write observation notes; don't try to be perfect, just capture visible issues.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Axial coding - categorizing error notes into 5-6 broad categories - requires domain expertise and human iteration; vague categories like 'quality' are useless, specific ones like 'text-message markdown rendering' are actionable.
actionable: Use AI to propose initial categories from notes, then refine iteratively into specific, actionable buckets; avoid vague category names.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Use pivot tables to count error categories, revealing which failure modes are most frequent and which to prioritize, even if impact analysis suggests otherwise.
actionable: After categorizing all error notes, create a pivot table to count frequency by category; use both frequency and estimated impact to prioritize fixes.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: LLM-as-judge evals must use binary (true/false) classification, not Likert scales, because binary is easier to validate (only 2 alignment checks) and matches real business decisions.
actionable: Design LLM judge prompts to return boolean (true/false) for any eval rather than numeric scores; structure rubrics around 'failure present: yes/no'.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Measuring LLM judge quality via agreement metric alone is a trap - a judge that always predicts 'pass' achieves 90% accuracy on imbalanced data; measure TPR (true positive rate) and TNR (true negative rate) separately.
actionable: After building an LLM judge, compute confusion matrix and report TPR/TNR alignment separately; require high TPR and high TNR independently.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Product managers (domain experts) must lead error analysis; separating prompt-writing from PMs or outsourcing error analysis to engineers breaks the feedback loop and removes product taste.
actionable: Ensure the PM/domain expert (not engineers) conducts error analysis; expose prompts to PMs in admin interfaces so they can iterate directly on the system.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Some errors are easy to fix with code-based evals (e.g., markdown in text output), while others need LLM judges (e.g., handoff decisions); prioritize code-based evals for quick wins.
actionable: For each error category, assess whether it's fixable via code assertions (run in CI, low cost) or requires LLM judgment (run in monitoring, higher cost).
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: A mature production eval suite typically has several code-based evals running in CI and 1-2 LLM-based evals; run LLM evals weekly/monthly on sampled traces, not on every execution.
actionable: Set up automated code-based evals in CI; schedule weekly sampling of 50-100 production traces to run against LLM judges offline.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: LLM judges perform better when given explicit rubric examples and clear failure criteria; iterating on judge prompts is as important as iterating on the main system prompt.
actionable: Include 2-3 annotated examples in LLM judge prompts showing what constitutes pass vs. fail; version-control and A/B test judge prompts against your ground truth set.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Generic metrics like 'helpfulness' or 'conciseness' miss domain-specific failures; real-world production traces contain multi-turn conversations, tool calls, RAG, and multi-channel complexity that generic LLM judges cannot evaluate.
actionable: Never rely on pre-built generic metrics; build application-specific evals reflecting actual product rules and user expectations from your domain.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)

claim: Iterating on system prompts to fix eval failures is faster than training models; once you have a reliable eval, change the prompt, measure error rate on test cases, and repeat.
actionable: Build a curated test set of traces for priority issues; use evals to quickly measure impact of prompt changes before deploying.
source_title: How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step, No Hype)
